TX.name: Texas
TX.enhancedBacNote: BAC ≥ 0.15% elevates DWI to Class A misdemeanor and significantly increases punitive exposure in civil cases
TX.faultDetail: Texas follows the 51% rule: you can recover damages as long as you were not more than 50% at fault. Your award is reduced by your percentage of fault. If you were 51% or more at fault, you recover nothing.

punitives.cap: $200,000 or 2x economic damages (whichever is greater) for most cases; uncapped for intentional acts
punitives.notes: DWI with BAC ≥ 0.15% or prior DUI convictions significantly strengthens punitive argument. Courts treat highly elevated BAC as conscious indifference to the rights of others — the legal standard for punitives.

dramShop.notes: Texas has one of the strongest dram shop laws in the US. A bar, restaurant, or social host can be held liable if they served alcohol to a person who was 'obviously intoxicated to the extent that they presented a clear danger to themselves or others.' This adds a second defendant — often with deep pockets.

CA.name: California
CA.enhancedBacNote: BAC ≥ 0.16% triggers enhanced DUI penalties in CA. Civil cases benefit significantly — higher BAC = stronger punitive damages argument under Cal. Civ. Code § 3294
CA.faultDetail: California uses pure comparative fault — you can recover damages even if you were 99% at fault. Your award is reduced by your percentage of fault. This is victim-friendly: even partial fault doesn't bar recovery.

punitives.cap: No statutory cap — juries have full discretion
punitives.notes: California allows punitive damages when the defendant acted with malice, oppression, or fraud. Getting behind the wheel with a BAC above the legal limit has been consistently found to meet this standard by CA courts. Punitive awards in CA DUI cases have ranged from $250,000 to over $5 million.

dramShop.notes: California has very limited dram shop liability. Under § 25602, the sale of alcohol to an intoxicated person is generally NOT a basis for civil liability. Exception: furnishing alcohol to a minor (under 21). If the drunk driver was a minor served by an establishment, dram shop liability applies under § 25602.1.
dramShop.typicalAdded: N/A unless driver was a minor

FL.name: Florida
FL.enhancedBacNote: BAC ≥ 0.15% constitutes 'enhanced DUI' in Florida — mandatory minimum sentences increase and civil punitive damages exposure rises substantially
FL.solCitation: Fla. Stat. § 95.11(3)(a) — NOTE: Florida reduced SOL from 4 to 2 years in March 2023 (HB 837). Accidents before March 24, 2023 may still have 4-year window.
FL.faultDetail: IMPORTANT: Florida changed from pure comparative fault to modified comparative in March 2023 (HB 837). If you are 51% or more at fault, you cannot recover. For accidents before March 24, 2023, the old pure comparative fault rule may still apply — consult an attorney.

punitives.cap: 3x compensatory damages OR $500,000 — whichever is greater (some exceptions allow higher)
punitives.notes: Florida specifically addresses DUI in punitive damages law. Under Fla. Stat. § 768.736, if the defendant was DUI at the time of the accident, punitive damages are available without the usual procedural hurdle of showing a reasonable basis first. This is a significant advantage for victims.

dramShop.notes: Florida's dram shop law allows liability when alcohol is sold or given to a person who is 'habitually addicted to the use of any or all alcoholic beverages' or to someone under 21. Standard cases where a bar served an already-drunk patron have a higher bar in FL — but bar-hopping with documented orders at multiple establishments builds a strong case.

NY.name: New York
NY.enhancedBacNote: BAC ≥ 0.18% triggers 'Aggravated DWI' (VTL § 1192(2-a)) — enhanced criminal penalties and a significantly stronger case for civil punitive damages
NY.solCitation: N.Y. C.P.L.R. § 214 — NOTE: NY has a longer SOL than most states. This is an advantage, but don't wait — evidence deteriorates and witnesses disappear.
NY.faultDetail: New York uses pure comparative fault (CPLR Article 14-A). You can recover damages even if you were partially at fault. Your award is reduced by your percentage of fault, but you are never fully barred from recovery based on fault alone.
NY.court: Supreme Court (NY's trial court of general jurisdiction — not to be confused with federal Supreme Court)

punitives.cap: No statutory cap — jury determines amount
punitives.notes: New York allows punitive damages in DUI cases where the conduct shows a conscious disregard for the rights of others. Courts look at the level of intoxication, prior DUI history, and whether the defendant attempted to flee. Punitive awards have reached into the millions in high-profile NY DUI cases.

dramShop.statute: N.Y. Gen. Oblig. Law § 11-101 (Dram Shop Act)
dramShop.notes: New York's Dram Shop Act is broad and well-established. Bars, restaurants, and social hosts can be held liable for injuries caused by someone to whom they unlawfully sold or gave alcohol. 'Unlawfully' includes serving a visibly intoxicated person. New York also has specific rules about serving minors. Multiple-bar evenings create strong dram shop liability exposure.

GA.name: Georgia
GA.enhancedBacNote: Georgia treats BAC ≥ 0.15% as 'less safe' DUI with enhanced penalties. In civil cases, higher BAC significantly bolsters punitive damages claims — Georgia has uncapped punitive damages for DUI cases.
GA.faultDetail: Georgia uses a 50% bar rule: you can recover as long as you were less than 50% at fault. If you were 50% or more at fault, you cannot recover. Your award is reduced proportionally by your fault percentage.

punitives.cap: UNCAPPED for DUI cases — this is significant
punitives.notes: Georgia is one of the few states with uncapped punitive damages in DUI cases. Under O.C.G.A. § 51-12-5.1(f), when harm is caused by specific intent to harm OR by DUI, punitive damages are NOT subject to the standard $250,000 cap. This creates enormous leverage in DUI civil cases — defendants and their insurers know there's no ceiling on exposure.

dramShop.notes: Georgia's dram shop law holds establishments liable that knowingly sell or furnish alcohol to a person who is in a state of noticeable intoxication, knowing that the person will soon be driving. 'Knowing' the person will drive is key — evidence of the drunk driver's car being visible in the parking lot has been held sufficient.

IL.name: Illinois
IL.enhancedBacNote: BAC ≥ 0.16% constitutes 'aggravated DUI' in Illinois — mandatory minimum sentences apply and civil exposure for punitive damages significantly increases
IL.faultDetail: Illinois follows the 51% bar rule: if you were 51% or more at fault, you cannot recover. If less than 51% at fault, your award is reduced by your fault percentage. Illinois courts take comparative fault seriously — documentation of the other driver's intoxication is critical.

punitives.cap: No statutory cap — but courts apply proportionality review
punitives.notes: Illinois allows punitive damages in DUI civil cases when the defendant acted with actual malice, deliberate violence, or conscious disregard for the rights of others. Courts consistently hold that driving with a BAC above the legal limit — especially a significantly elevated BAC — meets the conscious disregard standard.

dramShop.notes: Illinois has one of the strongest dram shop laws in the country. The Dram Shop Act imposes strict liability — meaning the vendor doesn't need to have known the person was intoxicated. If alcohol was sold and the person caused injury while intoxicated, the seller is liable up to statutory limits ($86,319 per person for injury — adjusted every 2 years for inflation).

AZ.name: Arizona
AZ.bacLimit: 0.08%; Extreme DUI at 0.15%; Super Extreme DUI at 0.20%
AZ.enhancedBacNote: Arizona has one of the most detailed BAC tiering systems in the US: Standard DUI (0.08–0.14%), Extreme DUI (0.15–0.19%), Super Extreme DUI (0.20%+). Each tier carries escalating criminal penalties and dramatically increases civil punitive damages exposure.
AZ.faultDetail: Arizona uses pure comparative fault. You can recover even if you were partially at fault — your award is reduced proportionally. There is no bar on recovery based on your own fault percentage.

punitives.cap: No statutory cap — courts apply proportionality
punitives.notes: Arizona allows punitive damages when the defendant's conduct was guided by evil mind — meaning conscious pursuit of a course of conduct knowing that it creates a substantial risk of significant harm to others. Driving at 0.15%+ BAC (Extreme DUI) has been found to satisfy this standard by Arizona courts. Super Extreme DUI (0.20%+) almost guarantees punitive damages.

dramShop.notes: Arizona imposes liability on liquor licensees who sell alcohol to a person when the licensee knew or should have known the person was intoxicated, or if the person was a minor. Arizona courts have also found social host liability in some cases involving minors. For bar-hopping DUI accidents, the dram shop claim against the last establishment served is often the strongest.

OH.name: Ohio
OH.duiStatute: O.R.C. § 4511.19 (OVI — Operating a Vehicle Under the Influence)
OH.bacLimit: 0.08%; 0.17% triggers 'high test' OVI with enhanced penalties
OH.enhancedBacNote: Ohio calls DUI 'OVI' (Operating a Vehicle Impaired). BAC ≥ 0.17% triggers 'high test' mandatory minimums. In civil cases, BAC at this level substantially strengthens punitive damages arguments under O.R.C. § 2315.21.
OH.faultDetail: Ohio follows the 51% rule under O.R.C. § 2315.33: if you were 51% or more at fault, you cannot recover. If less than 51%, your award is reduced proportionally. Ohio courts weigh traffic violation evidence heavily when determining fault.

punitives.cap: 2x compensatory damages OR $350,000 — whichever is greater (capped at $10,000 for small compensatory awards)
punitives.notes: Ohio caps punitive damages but the 2x compensatory rule means serious injury cases with high economic damages can still yield substantial punitive awards. OVI cases — especially with BAC at 0.17%+ — consistently meet the 'actual malice' standard required for punitive damages in Ohio.

dramShop.notes: Ohio's dram shop law imposes liability on liquor permit holders who knowingly sell intoxicating liquor to a noticeably intoxicated person, if that sale causes injury. 'Knowingly' is the key word — Ohio courts look at whether the bartender observed signs of intoxication (slurred speech, unsteady gait, smell of alcohol). Bar tabs, surveillance footage, and witness statements are critical evidence.
